coredata.dc:title: Enhanced thermoelectric performance by constructing PEDOT:PSS/graphene quantum dots/single-walled carbon nanotube multilayer films
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Fei-Peng
Zhang, Hui
Yao, Jun-An
Chen, Si-Ying
Xiao, Jin-Kun
Fu, Ping
Zhang, Yun-Fei
coredata.dc:description: Flexible thermoelectric materials are important as self-powered systems in wearable and flexible thermoelectric devices; to this end, using single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) is advantageous for enhancing the overall thermoelectric performance. In this study, to further enhance the thermoelectric properties of SWCNTs and overcome the weak interfacial interaction between hydrophilic poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) and hydrophobic pristine SWCNTs, a graphene quantum dots (GQDs) intermediate layer is used to couple PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs. Multilayer films comprising PEDOT:PSS, GQDs, and SWCNTs are constructed and prepared by successively drip-coating the GQDs and PEDOT:PSS on the SWCNTs film surface. Analysis of the interfacial morphology reveals that the hydrophilic GQDs improve interfacial adhesion between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs. Compared to a PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT bilayer film, the electrical conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT multilayer films is enhanced, while the Seebeck coeﬃcient is slightly lower. Meanwhile, the thermal conductivity is significantly decreased due to the increased interfacial phonon scattering. The minimum in-plane thermal conductivity for the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT films is 7.4 W‧m−1‧K−1, resulting in an enhanced figure of merit of 1.96 × 10−3 at 298 K. Thus, the GQDs intermediate layer is beneficial for coupling PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs layers. The strategy employed herein offers novel insights into the preparation of high-performance SWCNTs-based thermoelectric materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEDOT:PSS
Graphene quantum dots
Single-walled carbon nanotubes
Multilayer
Thermoelectric properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the preparation procedure for the P/G/S films; (b) Photographs of the P/G/S films; (c) WCA of the PEDOT:PSS, p-SWCNTs, and b-GQDs/SWCNT films.
Film surface of bilayer films (a) P/S (25:100) and (b) P/S (50:100), and multilayer films (c) P/G/S (25:2:100) and (d) P/G/S (50:2:100).

Cross-sectional and partially enlarged upper, middle, and lower-area images of bilayer films (a, b) P/S (25:100) and (c, d) P/S (50:100) and multilayer films (e, f) P/G/S (25:2:100) and (g, h) P/G/S (50:2:100).
(a) Raman spectra and (b) carbon radial breathing mode of p-SWCNTs, PEDOT:PSS, P/S (50:100), and P/S (50:2:100) films.
(a) Electrical conductivity, (b) Seebeck coefficient, and (c) power factor of the prepared films.
Schematic of energy levels and carrier transport: (a) P/G/S multilayer films and (b) P/S bilayer films.
Carrier concentration and carrier mobility of the prepared films at 298 K.
Figure of merit (ZT) and in-plane thermal conductivity of SWCNTs-based films at 298 K.
The TE properties of carbon nanotubes based TE composite films with different morphologies.

head.ce:title: Enhanced thermoelectric performance by constructing PEDOT:PSS/graphene quantum dots/single-walled carbon nanotube multilayer films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible thermoelectric materials are important as self-powered systems in wearable and flexible thermoelectric devices; to this end, using single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) is advantageous for enhancing the overall thermoelectric performance. In this study, to further enhance the thermoelectric properties of SWCNTs and overcome the weak interfacial interaction between hydrophilic poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) and hydrophobic pristine SWCNTs, a graphene quantum dots (GQDs) intermediate layer is used to couple PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs. Multilayer films comprising PEDOT:PSS, GQDs, and SWCNTs are constructed and prepared by successively drip-coating the GQDs and PEDOT:PSS on the SWCNTs film surface. Analysis of the interfacial morphology reveals that the hydrophilic GQDs improve interfacial adhesion between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs. Compared to a PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT bilayer film, the electrical conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT multilayer films is enhanced, while the Seebeck coeﬃcient is slightly lower. Meanwhile, the thermal conductivity is significantly decreased due to the increased interfacial phonon scattering. The minimum in-plane thermal conductivity for the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT films is 7.4 W‧m−1‧K−1, resulting in an enhanced figure of merit of 1.96 × 10−3 at 298 K. Thus, the GQDs intermediate layer is beneficial for coupling PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs layers. The strategy employed herein offers novel insights into the preparation of high-performance SWCNTs-based thermoelectric materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Low power wearable devices, like mobile phones, smart watches, and wireless sensors are accelerating into people's life, and there is an urgent need to develop micro- or milli-watt self-powered system to replace the traditional rechargeable battery [1–3] . Recently, research on flexible thermoelectric (TE) materials has attracted attention as they allow direct interconversion between body heat and green electricity, rendering them advantageous for use as a self-powered system in wearable and flexible TE devices [4–6] . The energy conversion efficiency of TE materials is typically quantified by the dimensionless figure of merit (ZT), defined as σS 2 T/κ, where σ, S, T, and κ are the electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, absolute temperature, and thermal conductivity, respectively [7–9] . For materials with low κ or in-plane κ is difficult to be precisely determined, ZT is always replaced by the power factor (PF=S 2 σ). Therefore, for a high ZT, TE materials should possess high values of S and σ, and a low value of κ. However, these parameters exhibit a strong interdependency and are associated with carrier transport; the typical inverse proportionality of S and σ limits improvements to the TE performance [10–12].

Among the various flexible TE materials, carbon nanotube (CNT)-based composites have invited considerable attention owing to their excellent electrical conductivity, high charge carrier mobility, low weight, as well as mechanical robustness and stability [13–19]. Many studies have focused on fabricating CNT composites with enhanced TE properties by incorporating polymers [20,21], Bi2Te3 nanocrystals [22], ionic liquids [23], fullerene [24], and graphene or graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [25–27] into the CNT matrix to increase phonon scattering and decrease the thermal conductivity. For example, Zhao et al. prepared three-dimensional porous graphene/multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) composites with an ultra-low κ (~0.056 W‧m−1‧K−1) and a ZT of 0.001 at 300 K [25]. Zhou et al. fabricated polyethylenimine/SWCNT composites with a κ of 18 W‧m−1‧K−1, PF of 1197 μW‧m−1‧K−2, and ZT of 0.02 at 298 K [28]. Typically, the TE properties of composites not only depend on the constituents and their content, but also on their morphologies and structures [29–32]. Thus far, SWCNTs-based TE composites have been prepared with various morphologies and structures, including coral-like, porous, and layer-like [33–36]. Chen et al. reported a coral-like coating morphology for poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)/carbon nanotube (PEDOT/CNT) composites with drastically improved TE performance [33]. Lee et al. developed highly porous CNT foams exhibiting a low thermal conductivity of 0.17 W‧m−1‧K−1, PF of 1.0 μW‧m−1‧K−2, ZT of 1.75 × 10−3 at 298 K [34]. Grunlan et al. reported a multilayer MWCNT/PEDOT thin film with a PF of 155 μW‧m−1‧K−2 fabricated via layer-by-layer assembly [35]. Cho et al. prepared a 40-repeating bilayer polyaniline (PANi)/double-walled CNT-graphene oxide thin film via layer-by-layer assembly. The film was reduced by L-ascorbic acid, and exhibited an electrical conductivity of 950 S/cm, Seebeck coeﬃcient of 115 μV/K, and a maximum PF of 1260 μW‧m−1‧K−2 [36]. Song et al. fabricated PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT bilayer TE films by spin-casting, and reported a maximum PF of 21.1 μW‧m−1‧K−2 at 300 K [37]. However, the prepared PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT films were rigid due to the use of a glass substrate. Shi et al. reported SWCNT/PEDOT:PSS/PANi multilayer TE films with an optimized PF of 12.3 μW‧m−1‧K−2 by electrochemical polymerization, with controllable oxidation degree and thickness of the PANi layer [38]. Seki et al. reported PEDOT/SWCNT heterogeneous TE bilayers with an optimized PF of 13.9 μW‧m−1‧K−2 by electrochemical polymerization [39]. However, there is a restriction to the size of the thin film prepared by electrochemical polymerization, imposed by the size of the electrode. Recently, our group fabricated bilayer-like structures of GQDs and SWCNTs films; the b-GQDs/SWCNT interfacial structure not only improved carrier transport to increase the electrical conductivity, but also caused phonon scattering to reduce the in-plane thermal conductivity. The obtained b-GQDs/SWCNT film showed a minimum κ of 38.0 W‧m−1‧K−1 and maximum PF of 51.2 μW‧m−1‧K−2, resulting in an enhanced ZT of 4.0 × 10−4 at 298 K [40]. However, this reported κ value is still very high. Furthermore, it remains a challenge to develop a simple method for the fabrication of multilayered SWCNTs-based composites with enhanced TE performance.

In this study, GQDs were used as an intermediate layer to couple a PEDOT:PSS layer and SWCNTs layer, and PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT (P/G/S) multilayer films were prepared. The prepared films were analyzed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and Raman spectroscopy to determine the effect of the GQDs intermediate layer on the TE performance of the multilayer films.

PEDOT:PSS (Clevios PH1000, PEDOT:PSS mass ratio of 1:2.5 by weight with 1.0–1.3% solid content in water) was purchased from Heraeus Holding GmbH (Leverkusen, Germany). Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Shenzhen Hongdachang Evolution Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). GQDs were prepared according to the reference [37]. SWCNTs (diameter<3 nm, purity>85%) were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane (0.22 µm pore size and 25 mm radius) was obtained from Shanghai Xingya Purification Material Factory (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were the analytical grade and were directly used without further purification.

Firstly, b-GQDs/SWCNT (2:100) was prepared according to the reference and details are shown in Supporting Information [37]. Then b-GQDs/SWCNT (2:100) was fixed on the polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) sheet with the adhesive tape, and PEDOT: PSS solution was dripped onto the surface of b-GQDs/SWCNT with a rubber dropper. Finally, the P/G/S film was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The samples with PEDOT: PSS and SWCNTs mass ratios of 25:100, 50:100 and 75:100 were denoted as P/G/S (25:2:100), P/G/S (50:2:100), and P/G/S (75:2:100), respectively. The initial thickness of P/G/S prepared by drip coating method is about 25 ± 3 µm. For comparison, pristine SWCNTs (p-SWCNTs) film, PEDOT:PSS film, and bilayer PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT are prepared (details are shown in Supporting Information).
The characterization methods and equipment descriptions are listed in the Supplementary Material.

Fig. 1(a) illustrates the preparation procedure for the P/G/S films, involving solution drip-coating. The films were prepared by successively drip-coating GQDs and PEDOT:PSS on the surface of SWCNTs film. The TEM results showed that the p-SWCNTs nanotubes aggregated into bundles (Fig. S1(a)) and the size of GQDs was about 15 ± 5 nm in diameter (Fig. S1(b)). Fig. 1(b) shows the prepared P/G/S film is self-standing and exhibits excellent flexibility. Fig. 1(c) shows that PEDOT:PSS is hydrophilic, with a water contact angle (WCA) of 61°, because PEDOT:PSS comprises a hydrophobic PEDOT core and hydrophilic PSS shell [41]. However, the p-SWCNTs film is hydrophobic, with a WCA of 115°. To make the surface of the p-SWCNTs film hydrophilic, a GQDs solution was drip-coated on the film surface. The TEM results showed that some dots attached on the surface of the nanotubes (Fig. S1(c)), which may be due to the aggregation of the GQDs via π-π stacking. Furthermore, the dots on the nanotubes had a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. S1(d)), corresponding to the
( 1
1
2
̅
0 )
lattice fringes of GQDs. Fig. 1(c) showed that the prepared b-GQDs/SWCNT films were hydrophilic, with WCAs of 29° and 25° for films with b-GQDs/SWCNT ratios of 2:100 and 20:100, respectively. Because GQDs contain abundant hydrophilic functional groups like -OH and -COOH, they can render the surface of SWCNTs hydrophilic. Therefore, the PEDOT:PSS solution can be uniformly coated on the surface of the b-GQDs/SWCNT films.

FE-SEM was performed to characterize the surface morphologies of the P/S bilayer films and P/G/S multilayer films. The FE-SEM images in Fig. 2(a, b) show that the surface of the P/S bilayer films is rough, and the roughness increases with the PEDOT:PSS content. This may occur because PEDOT:PSS, which has a hydrophilic PSS shell, is incompatible with the hydrophobic SWCNTs; further, the PEDOT:PSS particles aggregate on the SWCNTs surface, as shown in the high-magnification image in the inset of Fig. 2(a, b) [42–45]. Fig. 2(c, d) shows that the surface of the P/G/S films is wrinkled and smooth. The high-magnification images in the insets of Fig. 2(c, d) further confirm that the surfaces of the P/G/S (25:2:100) and P/G/S (50:2:100) films are smooth, suggesting that the chain alignment of PEDOT may improve by interaction with the GQDs intermediate layer [46]. The GQDs intermediate layer not only reduces the surface roughness of the p-SWCNTs, but also enhances the wettability of PEDOT:PSS to the SWCNTs [41].

Fig. 3 shows the cross-section morphology and PEDOT:PSS distribution in the prepared bilayer and multilayer films. Fig. 3(a, c, e, and g) shows that the thickness of the prepared P/S and P/G/S films is approximately 25 µm. The high-magnification images in Fig. 3(b) and (d) show that the upper layers of the P/S (25:100) and P/S (50:100) films are dense, while the lower layers are porous, with distinct tube bundles, suggesting poor interaction between the hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS and hydrophobic SWCNTs. Fig. 3(f) shows that the upper, middle, and lower layers of the P/G/S (25:2:100) films were all coated by PEDOT:PSS, and the the lower layers were less porous. This might be ascribed to the reason that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) introduced worked as an intermediate layer to couple the hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS and hydrophobic SWCNTs layers, which made SWCNTs bundles easily coated by PEDOT:PSS [46]. Furthermore, Fig. 3(g, h) shows that a higher PEDOT:PSS content results in each layer of the P/G/S (50:2:100) films becoming denser and smoother. This suggests that PEDOT:PSS adheres to the surface of the SWCNTs via the GQDs layer, and that the interface between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs is no longer distinct due to the coupling interaction of the GQDs. Thus, the P/S bilayer film is composed of a dense PEDOT:PSS upper layer and a loose SWCNTs bottom layer. On the other hand, the P/G/S multilayer film is composed of a dense PEDOT:PSS upper layer, dense PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT middle layer, and less-dense SWCNTs bottom layer.

Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of the p-SWCNTs, PEDOT:PSS, P/S, and P/G/S films. For the p-SWCNTs as well as the P/S and P/G/S films, the G peaks around 1595 cm−1 in Fig. 4(a) can be assigned to the carbon ordering region; The D peaks (1353 cm−1) assigned to the disordered region are not distinct, suggesting that the ordered structures of the SWCNTs are not destroyed during preparation. In Fig. 4(a), PEDOT:PSS exhibits three main peaks at 1265 cm−1, 1370 cm−1, and 1429 cm−1, which are assigned to C-C inter-ring stretching, C-C single-bond stretching, and CC symmetrical stretching vibrations, respectively. The Cα = Cβ peak is assigned to the symmetric vibration of the thiophene ring in PEDOT chains [41,42]. In Fig. 4(b), the vibration peak at approximately 142 cm−1 is assigned to the carbon radial breathing mode (RBM). The P/G/S (50:2:100) and P/S (50:100) films do not exhibit distinct RBM peaks, which could be due to the reason that the dense PEDOT:PSS layer that formed on the surface of the SWCNTs disturbed the carbon radial respiration signal of the SWCNTs. In Fig. 4(c), the G peak of the SWCNTs is blue-shifted from 1594 cm−1 to 1595 cm−1 for the P/S film and to 1598 cm−1 for the P/G/S film, compared to the p-SWCNTs. The smaller G-band blue-shift for the P/S film indicates a weak interaction between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs in the film, whereas the larger G-band blue-shift for the P/G/S film indicates that the interaction between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs is enhanced by the GQDs intermediate layer in the film [46]. In Fig. 4(d), a sharper and more intense Cα = Cβ peak appears for the P/G/S (50:2:100) film compared to the P/S (50:100) film. Further, the Cα = Cβ peak is blue-shifted from 1431 cm−1 to 1436 cm−1 for the former compared to the latter upon the addition of the GQDs intermediate layer, suggesting that GQDs improve the interaction between PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs [35].

Table 1 shows the carrier concentration and carrier mobility of the prepared films. The electrical conductivity (σ) can be expressed by σ = nqµ, where n is the carrier concentration, q is the elementary charge, and μ is the carrier mobility. σ is proportional to n and µ [47]. In this study, with the addition of PEDOT:PSS, the carrier concentration of P/S and P/G/S series films is increased while the carrier mobility shows the opposite trend. It might be ascribed to the reason that high carrier concentration PEDOT:PSS increases the carrier concentration of the composite films and interfacial scattering decreases the carrier mobility. Notably, the carrier concentration of the P/G/S series films are higher than that of the P/S series films while the carrier mobility shows the opposite trend, when the concentration of PEDOT:PSS is the same. This difference may arise from the fact that the GQDs intermediate layer enhances the interaction between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs, and improves the chain alignment of PEDOT, thereby improving carrier transmission. Therefore, the electrical conductivity of the P/G/S series films distinctly increases.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and PF of the prepared films. In Fig. 5 (a), the electrical conductivities of p-SWCNTs and the PEDOT:PSS film are 136.6 S/cm and 1060.2 S/cm, respectively. For the P/S films, when the mass ratio of PEDOT:PSS to the SWCNTs was increased from 25:100 to 75:100, the electrical conductivity increased from 286.2 ± 13 S/cm for the P/S (25:100) film to 408.6 ± 11.7 S/cm for the P/S (75:100) film. This is attributed to the high conductivity of PEDOT:PSS, which bridged the gap with the p-SWCNTs film, forming a dense conductive path [39]. For P/G/S films with a constant GQDs content, the electrical conductivity of the films increased from 555.9 ± 12.3 S/cm for the P/G/S (25:2:100) film to 775.3 ± 18.9 S/cm for the P/G/S film (75:2:100) with increasing mass ratio of PEDOT:PSS to the SWCNTs. Notably, when the PEDOT:PSS content was maintained constant, the electrical conductivity of the P/G/S series films are significantly higher than that of the P/S series films, possibly because the GQDs layer enhances interfacial contact between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs and improves the chain alignment of PEDOT:PSS, as shown in the FE-SEM images (Fig. 2(d)).

In Fig. 5(b), the Seebeck coefficients of the p-SWCNTs film and PEDOT:PSS are 54.7 μV/K and 15.9 μV/K, respectively. Compared with the p-SWCNTs film, the Seebeck coefficient of the multilayer films decreased. The Seebeck coefficient of the P/S films decreased from 42.1 μV/K for the P/S (25:100) film to 34.2 μV/K for the P/S (75:100) film, whereas that of the P/G/S films decreased from 29.1 μV/K for the P/G/S (25:2:100) film to 23.6 μV/K for the P/G/S (75:2:100) film. This is because the carrier concentration of these multilayer films increases compared to that of the p-SWCNTs film, and the Seebeck coefficient of these multilayer films varies in an opposite manner with the carrier concentration [48,49]. In Fig. 5(c), the PF values of the p-SWCNTs film and PEDOT:PSS film are 40.9 ± 0.9 μW‧m−1‧K−2 and 26.6 ± 1.3 μW‧m−1‧K−2, respectively. The PF values of the P/S and P/G/S multilayer films are higher than that of the p-SWCNTs film, due to the considerably enhanced electrical conductivity and slightly decreased Seebeck coeﬃcient. The maximum PF of the P/S films is 50.7 ± 3.9 μW‧m−1‧K−2 for a 25:100 P/S ratio, while that of the P/G/S films is 48.8 ± 3.3 μW‧m−1‧K−2 for a 50:2:100 P/G/S ratio. Therefore, it is advantageous to prepare P/S and P/G/S multilayer films as they improve the TE properties of SWCNTs film without considering the thermal conductivity.

Fig. 6 shows the interfacial energy levels of the multilayer P/G/S and bilayer P/S films. The work function and valence band of PEDOT:PSS, GQDs, and the SWCNTs were obtained by a UPS test, and the optical band gaps were obtained by UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy (calculation details are shown in the Supporting Information). The work functions of the SWCNTs, GQDs and PEDOT:PSS were 4.76 eV, 4.90 eV, and 5.19 eV, respectively. Therefore, in the multilayer P/G/S films, holes carriers are transported from PEDOT to the GQDs and then to the SWCNTs, resulting in increased hole concentration in the multilayer composites and a correspondingly decreased Seebeck coefficient. In the bilayer P/S films, holes carriers are transported from PEDOT:PSS to the SWCNTs because the work function of PEDOT: PSS (5.19 eV) is higher than that of the SWCNTs (4.76 eV). The hole carrier concentration of the P/S film increases with increasing PEDOT:PSS content, resulting in a decreased Seebeck coefficient, which is consistent with Hall effect results. There may also be an energy filter effect in the multilayer composites, according to the diagram in Fig. 6. However, the Seebeck coefficient of the multilayer composites is lower than that of the p-SWCNTs. This is because the energy filter effect cannot compensate for an increase in the number of holes after PEDOT:PSS is added, which is consistent with the Hall effect results.

Fig. S8 shows that the specific heat capacity (C
P ) of the prepared multilayer films changes slightly in the range of 273–323 K. Table 2 shows the C
P , density, thermal diffusion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and ZT values of the prepared films at 298 K. The total thermal conductivity can be expressed as κ total = κ e+ κ l, where κ e and κ l are the thermal conductivities of electrons and lattices, respectively [19]. The κ e for the SWCNTs-based films can be calculated according to the Wiedemann–Franz law (κ e = LσT, where L is the Lorentz number (2.44 × 10−8 W‧Ω‧K−2) and T is the temperature). In order to test the thermal conductivity precisely, the heat resistance between the P/G/S film and the heat source or sink is avoided by coating heat-conducting paste [50]. The P/G/S (75:2:100) film exhibits the highest σ (775.3 S/cm), and its κe is approximately 0.6 W‧m−1‧K−1 at 298 K. Consequently, the κ e of the other prepared films is lower than 0.6 W‧m−1‧K−1, and κ l primarily contributes to the thermal conductivity of the film whereas κ e makes a secondary contribution. For the P/G/S films, the thermal diffusion coefficient distinctly decreases with increasing PEDOT: PSS content due to the specific heat capacity of PEDOT: PSS [51]. The optimal κ and ZT values for the P/G/S films are 7.4 W‧m−1‧K−1 and 1.96 × 10−3, respectively, obtained with the P/G/S (50:2:100) film. Compared to the P/S films, P/G/S films have higher electrical conductivity and lower thermal conductivity, which means that the introduction of GQDs layer made the decoupling between electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity. Therefore, the strategy whereby an SWCNTs layer is coated with low-κ materials such as PEDOT:PSS and GQDs is effective to further improve the TE properties of SWCNTs-based materials.

Table 3 summarizes the TE properties of CNTs-based TE materials with various morphologies reported in recent years. By regulating material constituent and fabrication process, various morphologies have been obtained for CNTs-based TE materials, including bilayer, porous and open-cell structures, particle/layered, hexagonal and polyhedral, as well as multilayer structures in this study. In Table 3, the films reported herein display the highest ZT values of 1.96 × 10−3 for multilayer P/G/S series, which are higher than those of most monolayer or bilayer SWCNTs reported. Although PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT multilayer composites have presented enhanced TE performance at room temperature, its ZT value is still not very impressive due to a dilemma between the electrical conductivity and the Seebeck coefficient. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop new multilayer PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/SWCNT composites with simultaneously improved electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient by increasing micro-structural order of PEDOT polymer chains and constructing multi-interface to increase energy filtering effect and further improve the ZT value.

In summary, multilayer P/G/S films were successfully prepared by successively drip-coating GQDs and PEDOT:PSS on the surface of SWCNTs film. The hydrophilic GQDs acted as an intermediate layer to improve the interfacial adhesion between PEDOT:PSS and the SWCNTs. The results showed that holes are transported from the PEDOT: PSS layer to the GQDs layer and enter the SWCNTs layer, and that the carrier concentration of the multilayer films increases with increasing PEDOT:PSS content. The electrical conductivity the P/G/S films was superior to that of P/S bilayer films and monolayer films (p-SWCNTs film and PEDOT:PSS film). Meanwhile, the incorporation of GQDs led to a reduction in the thermal conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS/SWCNT due to the intrinsically low thermal conductivity and interfacial phonon scattering. The thermal conductivity of the P/G/S (50:2:100) film was 7.4 W‧m−1‧K−1, with a maximum ZT of 1.96 × 10−3 at 298 K. Thus, multilayer P/G/S films can be used to enhance the TE properties of SWCNTs-based materials, and the strategy employed herein can be used for the preparation of flexible TE materials.

Feipeng Du: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Junan Yao: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Si-Ying Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Jin-Kun Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Validation. Ping Fu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision. Yun-Fei Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803157).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164998.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing the thermoelectric performance of single-walled carbon nanotube-conducting polymer nanocomposites
